name: Fan-out amplification on high-cardinality events
trigger_shapes: event triggers a notification or action for every subscriber or follower of the source
pub/sub topic where the number of subscribers is unbounded or user-controlled
social graph operation (follow, post, like) that fan-outs to all connections
webhook delivery that sends to N registered endpoints on every event
batch job that generates one downstream task per row in an unbounded table
activation: An event from a high-cardinality source (a user with millions of followers, a topic with thousands of subscribers, a table with millions of rows) triggers a fan-out operation. The fan-out generates one unit of work per recipient. The total work is O(source_cardinality), not O(1). When a popular source fires frequently, aggregate fan-out load overwhelms the downstream processing capacity.
mechanism: User A has 5,000,000 followers. User A posts a tweet. The fanout service enqueues one notification job per follower: 5,000,000 jobs in seconds. Worker pool (100 workers) processes at 1,000 jobs/sec = 5,000 seconds (83 minutes) to drain. While draining, User A posts again: another 5,000,000 jobs added. Queue depth grows unbounded. Notifications for ordinary users (100 followers) arrive immediately; notifications for followers of popular users never arrive — or arrive hours late.

signal_sequence.signal: Fan-out queue depth grows during high-cardinality events. Average queue depth looks normal because most users are low-cardinality. P99 latency for notifications is not measured.

Notification delivery latency diverges: low-cardinality users receive instantly, high-cardinality-source followers wait minutes or hours. Queue depth spikes correlate with activity from popular accounts.
Fan-out queue becomes a persistent backlog. Worker fleet cannot keep up. Other event types sharing the same queue are also delayed. SLA for all notifications is breached.
Followers of popular accounts receive notifications hours after the event. System appears broken for high-traffic events precisely when reliability matters most (product launches, breaking news).

lag: minutes to hours (depends on source cardinality and worker throughput)
threshold_estimated: Fan-out events with cardinality > 10,000 recipients require explicit capacity planning. At 1M followers and 1,000 jobs/sec processing capacity, drain time = 1,000 seconds per event. A second event before the first drains produces unbounded queue growth. Twitter's celebrity problem demonstrated this at scale.
invisible_because: Fan-out logic is simple (one notification per follower) and correct for all unit tests. Load tests use synthetic users with equal, small follower counts. The high-cardinality case is never tested because creating 1M test followers is expensive. Queue depth alerts use average or sum, masking the spikes from individual high-cardinality events.
diagnostic: What is the maximum fan-out cardinality in production today (max followers, max subscribers, max rows)? Under a simulated high-cardinality event, what is the queue drain time? Is there a separate processing path for high-cardinality events (push model) vs low-cardinality (pull model)?
countermeasures: Use a pull model for high-cardinality sources: store the event once and let followers fetch on next request, rather than pushing to each follower's inbox.

Implement tiered fan-out: high-cardinality events go to a dedicated high-capacity queue; low-cardinality events go to the standard queue — prevents high-cardinality events from blocking low-cardinality notifications.
Cap fan-out at a configurable maximum per event (e.g., 10,000 recipients per batch); process the remainder asynchronously with lower priority.
Monitor queue depth by source cardinality bucket, not average — a 1M-follower event is invisible in averages.
Apply back-pressure at the event producer: rate-limit high-cardinality sources, or accept that their followers will receive notifications with measured latency SLA degradation.

sources.title: Twitter Engineering — Storing Data with Twitter
Martin Fowler — Event-Driven Architecture Fan-Out Pattern
